evidence: Both files implement near-parallel contract catalog pipelines (constants -> catalog specs -> validator) with separate local conventions instead of a shared contract-family boundary.

goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go validates bindings via namespace/action key mapping and formula checks, while goseg/protocol/contracts/startram_contracts.go validates by fixed length and `entries[0]` positional assumption.
Contract ID composition rules are encoded differently per file (protocol action formula path vs StarTram prefix+singleton assumption), increasing drift risk as new contracts are added.

Runtime structs are broad parameter bags that mix unrelated capabilities (for example `workflowRuntime` embeds container, urbit, snapshot, transition, sleep, DNS, and upload orchestration concerns in `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`; `importerRuntime` composes storage/upload/provision plus embedded docker runtime ops in `goseg/importer/importer.go`; `StartupRuntime` and `startBackgroundServicesRuntime` carry many callbacks in `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`).

Many call paths repeatedly construct/resolve default runtimes and seam-merge wrappers before actual work (`newWorkflowRuntime` + `resolveWorkflowRuntime` around small operations in `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`; `startBackgroundServices`/`defaultStartBackgroundServicesRuntime` runtime closure wiring in `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`; `runtimeSeams`/`buildRuntimeSeamBundle` function-field assembly in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`).

Multiple generic helper abstractions overlap in responsibility (typed transition abstraction plus mutation abstraction in `goseg/broadcast/transitions.go`, while runtime mutation and broadcasting already exist in `goseg/broadcast/state.go` and `goseg/broadcast/loop.go`), increasing indirection choices for similar state updates.

Runtime update logic imports the CLI verifier package path (`github.com/slsa-framework/slsa-verifier/v2/cli/slsa-verifier/verify`) inside production update code in `goseg/routines/version.go`, coupling core runtime behavior to a CLI-oriented dependency surface.

Docker SDK types and event/container primitives are used directly across several higher-level subsystems (`goseg/docker/network/network.go`, `goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go`, `goseg/routines/logstream/logs.go`) instead of being consistently hidden behind one adapter boundary, which broadens blast radius when SDK APIs change.
holistic_context.structure shows `goseg/config/` has 41 files (19 production, 22 test), indicating flat-directory overload above the >10-file mixed-concern threshold.
holistic_context.structure shows `goseg/shipworkflow/` has 45 files (23 production, 22 test), with lifecycle/runtime/newship/startram/urbit concerns co-located in one package.

holistic_context.structure shows `goseg/system/` has 42 files (19 production, 23 test), while this batch includes `apt.go` and `apt_test.go` that are maintenance/OS-package concerns mixed into a directory dominated by disk/wifi/system runtime features.

`goseg/config/bootstrap.go` performs process bootstrap and runtime path/env initialization, while `goseg/config/config.go` holds long-lived settings/state access and runtime context snapshot logic; these are distinct ownership boundaries but remain in the same flat package.

README sets a broad policy: deterministic tests for registration/version/upload/C2C changes (line 86), but the explicitly named CI gate scope is narrower and path-focused around upload/startram/protocol surfaces (lines 105-108), creating policy-vs-enforcement drift.

Critical governance is documented in prose-only obligations (README lines 82-101) with no executable mapping in these contract files themselves, so omissions in CI path filters can leave contract-family drift undetected.

Config mutation/parsing logic is split across two parallel models: `ConfPatch` + patch parsers in `goseg/config/config_update_schema.go` and direct JSON-key parsing/assignment in `goseg/structs/configs.go` (`UrbitDocker.UnmarshalJSON` switch).
Primitive parsing helpers are duplicated with near-identical behavior in both files (`parseBoolValue/parseIntValue/parseStringValue` vs `parseRequiredBool/parseRequiredInt/parseRequiredString`).

`goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go` combines command dispatch, transition-plan construction, persistence updates, container lifecycle actions, and publish side effects in one runtime file.

`goseg/system/wifi_test.go` and `goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint_test.go` each aggregate multiple distinct behavioral areas into very large single test files, reducing cohesion and making intent-by-section harder to maintain.

The code exposes multiple parallel entrypoints for the same behavior with different defaulting semantics, e.g. `SysLogStreamer`, `SysLogStreamerWithContext`, and `SysLogStreamerWithRuntime` in `goseg/routines/logstream/logs.go`, plus similar triads for docker log streaming.

Workflow APIs duplicate surface area via `RunTransitionedOperation` and `runTransitionedOperationWithRuntime` in `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`, forcing callers to choose between implicit runtime construction and explicit runtime injection.
Upload session APIs split closely related responsibilities across `SetUploadSession`, `OpenUploadEndpoint`, `VerifySession`, and `validateUploadRequest` in `goseg/importer/importer.go`.
Upload authorization is consistently enforced in the critical request path via `validateUploadRequest` checking both token headers and token authorization callback in `goseg/importer/importer.go`.
Session token verification includes rotation handling (`authz.IsRotated`) and updates stored token state, reducing drift between issued and accepted credentials in `goseg/importer/importer.go`.
authorization_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: Authorization checks are concentrated in importer upload flow; adjacent APIs like `VerifySession` expose weaker semantics (existence-only) that can be misused by future callers.

evidence: Error handling policy is split between critical and best-effort variants (`publishImportStatus` vs `publishImportStatusBestEffort`, `publishImportTransition` vs `publishImportTransitionBestEffort`) in `goseg/importer/importer.go`, with call-site choice determining propagation behavior.

Some parsing boundaries drop root cause detail (`parseUploadChunkMetadata` returns plain `invalid chunk index` / `invalid total chunk count`) while most neighboring functions wrap causes with `%w` in `goseg/importer/importer.go`.
Transition publishing uses policy-mediated error shaping in `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`, while other paths return direct wrapped errors, producing mixed failure contracts.
Acronym casing is inconsistent across related APIs: public `StartC2CCheck` vs internal `startC2cCheck` in `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`.

Method name `init()` on `startupOrchestrator` (`goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`) is easy to confuse with Go package `init` semantics and does not communicate phase intent as clearly as `initializePhases`/`runInitPhase`.
Mixed acronym styles appear in configuration and runtime naming (`C2cInterval` in `goseg/config/config_update_schema.go` vs `C2C` naming in runtime/startup code).
Sibling modules follow different acronym conventions for the same domain concept (`C2C` in startup/main paths vs `C2cInterval` in config patch schema).
Exported/internal API naming conventions are not uniformly applied for acronym-containing identifiers (`StartC2CCheck` vs `startC2cCheck`) within the same startup orchestrator subsystem.

Package-level `init()` in `goseg/main.go` performs embedded FS setup and mutates globals (`webContent`, `fileServer`, `initWebErr`), then startup validation later depends on `initWebErr` in `runBootstrapSubsystems`.

Package-level `init()` in `goseg/config/config.go` initializes runtime context, while `config.Initialize()` in `goseg/config/bootstrap.go` mutates additional global process state (`Ready`, global config, debug mode) behind `sync.Once`.
Runtime behavior depends on ambient process inputs (`os.Args` and `GS_BASE_PATH`) read during initialization paths in `goseg/config/bootstrap.go`, creating order-sensitive global state.

findings.summary: Contract-family governance is duplicated with divergent validation models across sibling modules
evidence: Both files define their own mini-framework for catalog entry construction and validation (`protocolActionContract`/`validateProtocolContractSpecs` vs `startramContract`/`validateStartramContractSpecs`).
Protocol contracts enforce semantic binding integrity (namespace/action -> expected ID + formula), while StarTram contracts enforce only prefix + singleton shape.
This creates two boundary contracts for the same package-level concern (`contracts` governance), so behavior for adding new contract families is inconsistent by file.
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared contract-family validator pattern in `contracts` (e.g., declarative family spec with reusable ID rule + binding rule hooks), then have both protocol and StarTram register through that common path so expansion uses one architectural contract.

findings.summary: StarTram validation is position-coupled (`entries[0]`) and drifts from key-based sibling validation
evidence: In `validateStartramContractSpecs`, correctness depends on `len(entries) == 1` and `entries[0].ID == APIConnectionError`.
In `validateProtocolContractSpecs`, validation is order-independent via key lookups over all entries.
This asymmetry means StarTram’s model will require rewrite when additional StarTram contracts are introduced, creating architecture drift between sibling contract domains.
findings.suggestion: Replace StarTram positional singleton checks with an order-independent expected-ID set/map (same structural style as protocol validation), and keep singleton behavior as policy data (expected count) rather than indexing assumptions.

findings.summary: Wide runtime bags collapse multiple domains into single mutable seam structs.
evidence: `workflowRuntime` embeds `RuntimeContainerOps`, `RuntimeUrbitOps`, and `RuntimeSnapshotOps` plus unrelated transition/sleep/network callbacks, so most call sites receive far more authority than needed.
`importerRuntime` combines storage, upload auth/session, provisioning, and docker volume/container operations in one composed runtime type, creating a broad implicit contract.
`startBackgroundServicesRuntime` exposes many per-service callbacks as one struct, then service specs dynamically pick fields, indicating role boundaries are data-driven but not type-enforced.
findings.suggestion: Split runtime bags into purpose-specific interfaces per use case (for example `ShipTransitionEmitter`, `ShipConfigStore`, `ContainerLifecycleOps`, `BackgroundServiceStarter`) and pass only the smallest interface each operation needs; keep composition only at top-level bootstrap wiring.

findings.summary: Repeated resolve/merge wrappers add indirection without changing behavior.
evidence: Many helper paths call `resolve*Runtime`/`new*Runtime` per operation (`runUrbitTransition`, `RunTransitionedOperation`, `WaitComplete`) before forwarding to core logic.

Startup path repeatedly merges defaults and overrides through multiple layers (`startupInitRuntimeWithDefaults`, `startupRuntimeWithDefaults`, `startBackgroundServicesRuntimeWithDefaults`) while most services use default bindings.
`WiFiRuntimeService.prepareForUse` re-resolves and validates runtime lazily on first use, adding runtime indirection for every public method rather than constructing a validated dependency once.
findings.suggestion: Collapse repetitive resolve layers into a single constructor per subsystem that returns a validated dependency object once; keep operation functions on already-resolved runtimes and reserve override-merging for tests/bootstrap only.

findings.summary: Broadcast state exposes two parallel mutation abstractions for similar work.
evidence: `ApplyBroadcastTransition` and `ApplyBroadcastMutation` both read state, mutate, write state, and optionally broadcast; callers can choose different paths for equivalent operations.

State runtime already contains mutation-supporting operations (`UpdateBroadcast`, `AddSystemTransitionError`, scheduling and broadcast methods), so separate transition/mutation APIs add overlap instead of a single authoritative write path.
findings.suggestion: Unify on one mutation contract (either typed transitions or explicit mutation callbacks) and route all state writes through one state-transaction API that handles update + notification consistently.

findings.summary: Production update flow depends on a CLI package namespace for SLSA verification.
evidence: `goseg/routines/version.go` imports `github.com/slsa-framework/slsa-verifier/v2/cli/slsa-verifier/verify` and uses `verify.VerifyArtifactCommand` during update checks.
Version subsystem is started as part of background startup services in `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`, so this dependency is part of core runtime boot behavior.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a small internal verification interface in the routines layer and wrap verifier implementation behind an adapter package that depends on stable library APIs (or isolated command invocation), keeping CLI-specific types out of core runtime code.

findings.summary: Direct Docker SDK type usage is spread across multiple high-level modules.
evidence: Network runtime directly imports several Docker API packages for container/network/volume operations.
Subsystem event handling depends directly on Docker event message types.
Logstream code imports Docker container log option types directly.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate Docker SDK interaction behind one internal adapter boundary (for example `dockerclient` plus domain DTOs) so orchestration, subsystem, and logstream packages consume internal contracts instead of raw Docker SDK types.

findings.summary: shipworkflow keeps multiple domains in one flat package, obscuring ownership boundaries
evidence: `goseg/shipworkflow/` is a 45-file directory with mixed concerns (newship, recovery, startram runtime, urbit operations, workflow helpers), and `lifecycle.go` is placed at top-level beside unrelated domain files.
`doc.go` describes broad lifecycle coordination, but package shape remains flat rather than reflecting those domain boundaries.
Top-level package test scaffolding (`coverage_smoke_test.go`) is colocated with all domain code, reinforcing one broad boundary rather than per-domain package seams.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create target folders `goseg/shipworkflow/lifecycle/`, `goseg/shipworkflow/urbit/`, `goseg/shipworkflow/startram/`, `goseg/shipworkflow/newship/`; (2) move `lifecycle.go` and its tests first into `lifecycle/` and expose only needed symbols through a thin package-level facade; (3) move urbit/startram/newship files in separate commits while preserving behavior; (4) update imports with `goimports -w goseg/shipworkflow/**/*.go goseg/**/*.go`; (5) validate with `go test ./goseg/shipworkflow/...` then `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: APT maintenance logic is placed in broad system package instead of a focused maintenance boundary
evidence: `apt.go` manages OS package update/upgrade parsing and global update state, a maintenance concern distinct from broader `system/` responsibilities.
`goseg/system/` has 42 files with multiple domains; `apt.go` sits alongside wifi/disk/runtime concerns rather than a dedicated package boundary.
Existing subpackage precedent (`goseg/system/maintenance/`) indicates directory boundaries are already used for maintenance-adjacent concerns, but apt functionality remains at top-level.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create `goseg/system/packages/` (or `goseg/system/maintenance/apt/`); (2) move `apt.go` and `apt_test.go` there first, then keep a temporary compatibility shim in `goseg/system` if needed; (3) relocate any apt-specific tests from top-level system test files into the new package; (4) run `goimports -w goseg/system/**/*.go goseg/**/*.go` to rewrite imports; (5) validate with `go test ./goseg/system/...` and `go test ./...` before removing shims.

findings.summary: config mixes bootstrap lifecycle orchestration with steady-state config API in one package root
evidence: `bootstrap.go` handles process initialization (`Initialize`, base-path/debug setup, config file creation/persistence flow), which is startup orchestration behavior.
`config.go` defines runtime context/state access primitives and domain setting snapshots, which are steady-state API responsibilities.
`goseg/config/` has 41 files in one directory, so bootstrap and state ownership are not encoded as directory/package boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create target folders `goseg/config/bootstrap/` and `goseg/config/state/`; (2) move `bootstrap.go` + `bootstrap_test.go` into `bootstrap/` first and keep exported wrapper functions in `config` during transition; (3) move runtime-context and snapshot primitives from `config.go` into `state/` in a second pass; (4) update call sites/imports with `goimports -w goseg/config/**/*.go goseg/**/*.go`; (5) validate with `go test ./goseg/config/...` then `go test ./...` and remove temporary wrappers.

findings.summary: Contract-ID and validation rules are duplicated per family instead of enforced through one boundary.
evidence: Protocol contract IDs are hand-built with string concatenation (`protocol_contracts.go` lines 21-23); StarTram does the same (`startram_contracts.go` line 21).
README requires typed contract APIs over raw string lookups (README line 99), but family files still encode ID-format logic locally.
Validation logic is parallel but separate across families (`validateProtocolContractSpecs` vs `validateStartramContractSpecs`), which makes compatibility invariants easy to drift across modules.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared contract-family registration API in `goseg/protocol/contracts` (for example, `RegisterActionContract` / `RegisterErrorContract`) that owns ID composition, prefix checks, and metadata validation once. Migrate `protocol_contracts.go` first to the shared builder, then `startram_contracts.go`, then remove per-file ID-format checks that are now centralized. Validate with `cd goseg && go test ./protocol/contracts ./protocol/actions`.

findings.summary: Documented contract-test obligations are broader than the explicitly described CI gate scope.
evidence: README mandates deterministic tests for registration/version/upload/C2C paths (line 86).
The named CI gate section is path-aware and enumerates upload/startram/protocol contract surfaces (lines 105-108), leaving no explicit enforcement mapping for all policy items stated above.
The contract files are core compatibility surfaces referenced by policy (README lines 93-95), so enforcement drift here weakens change safety.
findings.suggestion: Make policy-to-check mapping executable: add a table in docs (or gate script comments) mapping each policy item (registration/version/upload/C2C/StarTram) to concrete test targets and required changed-path triggers. Then extend `.github/scripts/check-upload-contract.sh` (or split by domain) so each policy item has at least one required test command. Validate with `cd goseg && go test ./protocol/contracts ./startram ./routines` and run the gate script locally.

findings.summary: Protocol governance is primarily documented in root README instead of near the contracts package.
evidence: README contains detailed package-specific contract governance and CI policy (lines 82-108) alongside unrelated install/uninstall sections (lines 110-146).
Directory profile indicates `goseg/protocol/contracts/` is a dedicated domain package (`file_count: 7`), but governance guidance for it is not colocated there.
This separation increases docs-vs-runtime drift risk because contract maintainers must edit root policy text outside their package boundary.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) Create `goseg/protocol/contracts/GOVERNANCE.md` as the canonical contract-family policy doc and move README lines 91-108 content there; (2) create `docs/architecture/backend-quality-gates.md` for CI gate rationale and command mapping; (3) reduce README backend section to short pointers. Move order: package-local governance first, then cross-package gate doc, then root README trim. Import/update + validation commands: `rg -n "protocol and error contract surfaces|Runtime Contract Gate|check-upload-contract" README.md docs goseg/protocol/contracts`, then `cd goseg && go test ./protocol/contracts ./protocol/actions`.

findings.summary: Config schema/parsing responsibilities are duplicated across independent layers, creating drift-prone design.
evidence: `goseg/config/config_update_schema.go` defines a full patch model (`ConfPatch`, section patch structs) plus parse/apply registry pipeline (`confPatchField`, `buildConfigPatch`, `parse*Value` helpers).
`goseg/structs/configs.go` separately defines `UrbitDocker.UnmarshalJSON` with a long key-switch that performs field-level parsing and assignment.
Both files encode key-level parsing semantics for config data, but in different abstractions and entry points, which increases synchronization cost.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate config-key parsing into one schema authority (either registry-driven field descriptors or typed decode structs with adapters), then make the other path consume that authority instead of re-encoding key handling.

findings.summary: Nearly identical primitive parsing helpers are reimplemented in two config-related modules.
evidence: `goseg/config/config_update_schema.go` has `parseBoolValue`, `parseIntValue`, and `parseStringValue` (lines ~333-357).
`goseg/structs/configs.go` has `parseRequiredBool`, `parseRequiredInt`, and `parseRequiredString` (lines ~330-355).
Both sets perform the same type assertions/conversions (including `float64` to `int`) with only naming/context differences.
findings.suggestion: Extract shared parse primitives into a single internal config parsing package and use typed wrappers only where error wording must differ.

findings.summary: Urbit operation runtime blends orchestration, domain rules, persistence, and side effects in one file.
evidence: `goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go` handles command routing (`runUrbitTransitionFromCommand`), transition step construction (`build*Steps`), direct persistence updates, Docker actions, Click integration, and broadcast publication.
The same file contains both generic transition framework glue (`runShipConfigTransition`) and specific business operations (`toggleBootStatus`, `schedulePack`, `linkMinIOLink`).

`goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go` similarly combines loop lifecycle control, event ingestion, and transition-failure policy handling, reinforcing an orchestration-file-as-catchall pattern.
findings.suggestion: Split operation runtime into focused units: command-to-transition mapping, reusable transition strategies, and side-effect adapters (docker/click/broadcast). Keep orchestration files as composition roots only.

findings.summary: Large test files mix multiple behavior domains, reducing test-module cohesion.
evidence: `goseg/system/wifi_test.go` includes nmcli parsing/runtime tests, C2C action parsing/dispatch tests, adapter constructor behavior, and wifi info loop lifecycle tests in one file (~753 lines).

`goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint_test.go` combines config text generation, validation utilities, process probe matching, shell command execution semantics, and router start/stop orchestration in one file (~946 lines).
These files read as multiple suites compressed into single modules, which makes navigation and future changes harder despite broad coverage.
findings.suggestion: Restructure into behavior-scoped test files (e.g., `wifi_nmcli_parse_test.go`, `wifi_c2c_service_test.go`, `accesspoint_router_runtime_test.go`, `accesspoint_process_probe_test.go`) with shared local helpers.

findings.summary: Import-time and boot-time global initialization are coupled through mutable package state, making startup order and tests fragile.
evidence: `main.init()` mutates `webContent/fileServer/initWebErr` and bootstrap later reads `initWebErr` via `ValidateConfig` callback.
`config.init()` seeds runtime context, while `config.Initialize()` separately mutates global config/readiness/debug mode under `sync.Once`.
Initialization reads process-global inputs (`os.Args`, `GS_BASE_PATH`) and writes package-global state, creating hidden dependencies across startup subsystems.
findings.suggestion: Replace package-level `init` side effects with explicit bootstrap constructors that return immutable runtime structs; pass those through `StartupOptions` so initialization order is encoded in call graph instead of global state.

findings.summary: Transition publishing and upload flow mix strict and best-effort error contracts at call sites, producing inconsistent failure behavior.
evidence: Importer exposes paired APIs with different semantics (`publishImportStatus` vs `publishImportStatusBestEffort`, `publishImportTransition` vs `publishImportTransitionBestEffort`).
Workflow transition emission uses policy-based error shaping (`transition.HandleTransitionPublishError`) while surrounding operations often return direct wrapped errors.
Call-site selection, not layer policy, decides whether transition failures are fatal.
findings.suggestion: Define one canonical transition error policy per workflow boundary (e.g., strict for user-visible state mutation, best-effort for telemetry only) and expose a single API per boundary that encodes that policy.

findings.summary: Multiple near-duplicate API variants expose the same capability with inconsistent defaulting and runtime resolution behavior.
evidence: Logstream exposes 3-level variants (`X`, `XWithContext`, `XWithRuntime`) with implicit `context.Background()` fallback in some paths.
Shipworkflow keeps both `RunTransitionedOperation` and `runTransitionedOperationWithRuntime`, splitting runtime ownership across call styles.
Importer exposes both command-style (`OpenUploadEndpoint`) and wrapper (`SetUploadSession`) plus separate `VerifySession` with weaker semantics.
findings.suggestion: Collapse each subsystem to a primary explicit API (runtime+context required) plus a thin compatibility shim layer marked deprecated; document one defaulting policy and remove duplicate behavioral entrypoints.

findings.summary: Acronym casing and lifecycle method names drift across adjacent startup/config code, reducing intent clarity.
evidence: Public `StartC2CCheck` and internal `startC2cCheck` use different acronym casing for the same concept.
`startupOrchestrator.init()` is semantically a startup phase executor, but name collides conceptually with Go's package `init` lifecycle.
Config field `C2cInterval` diverges from `C2C` naming used in runtime/startup APIs.
findings.suggestion: Adopt a repository-level acronym naming rule (e.g., preserve all-caps for known acronyms in identifiers) and rename startup phase functions to explicit verbs (`initialize`, `runInitPhase`) with compatibility refactors in one pass.

findings.summary: Startup and config modules form a style island where acronym conventions differ inside the same domain boundary.
evidence: Startup APIs consistently use `C2C` in exported names, while config schema/patch naming uses `C2c`.
The mismatch appears within directly collaborating modules (startup consumes config values), not isolated legacy code.
findings.suggestion: Normalize acronym casing end-to-end for C2C-related identifiers and JSON field mapping helpers, including migration helpers/tests where external JSON compatibility is required.
